title: Listening - Part 4: Driving Passenger Trains
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with Daniel, a train driver. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: My guest today is Daniel Morris, who drives passenger trains between several northern cities. Daniel, were you one of those children who loved trains? || Daniel: I liked travelling, but I did not collect model trains or memorise timetables. I was thinking about several different careers when our school arranged visits to local employers. [24]After being allowed to sit in a driver's cab and watch how carefully a journey was managed, I applied for railway training[/24]. It seemed both practical and responsible, which appealed to me. || Presenter: Did you learn to drive immediately? || Daniel: No, and that surprised me. Everyone wants to know what it feels like to move a train, but that came later. For months I studied rules, routes and what to do in unusual situations. [25]The greatest challenge in training was learning the position and meaning of hundreds of signals and signs[/25]. You need to recognise them quickly, even in poor visibility. || Presenter: What part of the work do passengers rarely notice? || Daniel: They see us arrive and depart, but not the amount of communication involved. Drivers are in regular contact with signallers and station staff. If there is a problem, [26]staying calm and exchanging accurate information matters more than doing anything dramatic[/26]. A small misunderstanding could cause a much longer delay. || Presenter: Speaking of delays, do passengers ever speak to you about them? || Daniel: Occasionally, particularly at stations where I change ends. People are normally reasonable if they know what is happening. [27]I have found that giving an honest explanation, even when there is no quick solution, reduces people's frustration[/27]. Silence makes a ten-minute wait feel much longer than it is. || Presenter: You also drive very early services. Are they easier because fewer people travel? || Daniel: The stations are quieter and there may be less traffic on the line. Yet getting up before four in the morning changes your whole routine. [28]I prepare for an early shift by going to bed unusually early rather than expecting coffee to keep me alert[/28]. It is not exciting advice, but tiredness is not acceptable in this job. || Presenter: Rail travel is often presented as an environmental choice. Does that affect how you feel? || Daniel: Yes, but lectures will not persuade everyone to leave their car at home. [29]I think people choose trains more often when the service is comfortable, dependable and easy to understand[/29]. Doing my part well is therefore a practical way of supporting more sustainable travel. || Presenter: Finally, what would you say to someone considering this career? || Daniel: Some people assume they cannot apply because they were not interested in engineering at school. That is not the most important thing. [30]You need concentration, willingness to follow procedures and the ability to remain patient when a journey changes[/30]. Technical knowledge is taught during training; the right attitude is harder to teach.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What led Daniel to apply for railway training?

options.A: A lifelong enthusiasm for model trains.
options.B: A school visit that showed him the job.
options.C: Advice from a relative on the railway.

questions.explanation: He applied after a school-arranged visit allowed him to observe the work from a driver's cab.

questions.question: What did Daniel find most demanding during training?
questions.explanation: He says learning hundreds of signals and signs was the greatest challenge.

questions.question: According to Daniel, what is essential when a problem occurs?
questions.explanation: He stresses calmness and accurate information exchange with railway staff.

questions.question: What has Daniel learned about passengers during delays?

options.A: They are less annoyed when informed honestly.
options.B: They prefer not to receive repeated announcements.
options.C: They expect drivers to solve problems immediately.

questions.explanation: He finds that honest explanations reduce frustration.

questions.question: How does Daniel manage early shifts?

options.A: He drinks coffee throughout the journey.
options.C: He asks to drive less busy routes.

questions.explanation: He goes to bed unusually early before a morning service.

questions.question: What does Daniel believe encourages sustainable rail travel?

options.C: Increasing the price of travelling by car.

questions.explanation: He thinks a dependable, comfortable, understandable service makes people choose trains.

questions.question: What does Daniel say is most important for applicants?

options.C: Personal qualities such as focus and patience.

questions.explanation: Technical knowledge can be taught, while concentration and patience are key qualities.
